title: 品牌在体育赞助中常犯的错误——以及如何正确操作
author: T. Bettina Cornwell
content:
Summary.   

    Companies spend billions sponsoring major sporting events, yet many still evaluate success primarily through exposure metrics such as logo visibility and immediate brand recall. New research suggests those measures increasingly misrepresent sponsorship 

      The two largest sports mega-events are coming to America. The United States, Canada, and Mexico are hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup matches, and Los Angeles is hosting the 2028 Olympics and Paralympics. These events are not just iconic competitions and multicultural celebrations—they’re also marketing platforms for the world’s largest brands.

      The entry fee to sponsor these events is hefty. Top-tier FIFA contracts cost $150 to $200 million, and most companies that spend big on a sponsorship subsequently invest about the same amount on advertising, events, and other so-called “activation” to leverage the sponsorship, pushing some sponsors’ spending over $400 million in the four-year cycle.

      Similarly, TOP Olympic contracts are estimated at over $200 million per cycle. Some brands sign long-term deals; according to media reports, Toyota committed $835 million for such a deal in 2015. Moreover, with two major events (Winter and Summer games) in a quadrennium, TOP Olympic sponsors tend to spend even more beyond the sponsorship fee.

      Despite rising investments, critics question the value of these sponsorships. In the early days of sports sponsorship, brands congruent with the sport sponsored (for example, a running shoe brand sponsoring a running event) were rewarded with recall. Lately, however, research has shown that fans have trouble recalling what brands are official sponsors. Additionally, today if your brand is a good match to a context, all your direct competitors are also a good match, and they are also likely sponsoring similar events reaching similar markets. Confusion and misattribution result.

      As a marketing academic, I’ve been studying the effectiveness of sports marketing sponsorships for more than 25 years. During that time, the tactics companies use to amplify their promotions have changed dramatically. Before the rise of YouTube and social media, for instance, the “before life” and afterlife of a televised Super Bowl ad was much smaller than it is today. But even as companies have found creative ways to increase the reach of their sponsorship spending, much of their thinking around the value of these efforts remains too narrow. They’re measuring returns the wrong way. And many companies rely on the same bland approach to sponsorships instead of leveraging their strengths or creating signature experiences that are more memorable.

      They’re missing an opportunity. In an AI era marked by fragile consumer trust, sport partnerships deliver authenticity and, through it, trust. Nielsen’s “Fans Are Changing the Game” report finds brand sponsorships in sports are more trusted than TV ads, influencer content, or social media posts. Moreover, partnering with sports holds the golden potential of reciprocity, as consumers say to themselves: I’ll buy from this brand because they support what’s important to me.

      In this article, drawing on my research studies and those with PhD students and collaborators, I’ll outline the ways companies are failing to take full advantage of the millions they spend on sport sponsorships—and suggest ways they can do it better.

      Why Exposure Became the Default Metric

      One of the basic mistakes companies make around sports sponsorships is limiting their measure of success to an outdated metric called “advertising value equivalency.”

      This metric has deep roots in legacy public relations evaluation. PR people would measure how much time a brand or logo was shown on the screen of a televised sporting event, compute the cost of buying equivalent ad time during that event, and use this as the key benchmark of the sponsorship’s return on investment. Today AI-enabled tracking systems can identify brand names and logos in venues and across broadcast, streaming, and social media. Exposures can be weighted for position at the heart of the action, clarity, and focus—and then aggregated across channels. The tracking has improved, but the goal remains the same: to translate visibility into a dollar value.

      In my work, I’ve argued that sponsors need to shift away from measuring ad equivalency and instead focus on authenticity and engagement. Consumers increasingly distrust overt marketing, but they respond positively when a brand appears to have a genuine relationship with a team, event, cause, or community they care about. To succeed in this environment, brands need to think about the sponsorship as part of an experience rather than simply a vehicle to transmit a message.

      To be clear, measurement firms recognize the shortcomings of both exposure-only measures and valuation-via-exposure measures. Companies including Nielsen, Relo Metrics, YouGov Sport, and Zoomph are all working on proprietary models to integrate additional data. For example, they are now measuring exposure-related likes and shares on social media to better calibrate the value of exposure. Nonetheless, exposure remains the dominant KPI—and that’s shortsighted.

      Research Demonstrates Why That’s a Problem

      Part of the reason companies should move beyond simple visibility metrics is because research shows consumers have a hard time recognizing which brands are actually sponsors—a problem that is, ironically, related to sponsorship expansion and success.

      We’ve found this problem is widespread for two reasons.

      First, brands naturally create ecosystems that become cluttered.

      International ecosystems (such as FIFA World Cup and the Olympics) attract brands that have global markets and deep pockets to afford mega sporting events. Brands with national markets choose national events, and regional brands choose regional events. By self-organizing into ecosystems, sponsoring brands form an “exposure pool” that includes many of the same brands—and often direct competitors. Sponsorship environments become crowded, and distinctions blur.

      When companies try to measure the effectiveness of their sponsorships in these crowded environments, they often measure in ways that create further confusion. Very often, companies don’t just ask consumers if they recall what companies were official sponsors; instead, they’ll ask them to pick among sets that include direct competitors.

      To understand how that affects recall, we conducted two studies using FIFA World Cup sponsorships from the 2024 games in Qatar. We compared recall of long-established sponsors such as Visa, Hyundai, and Budweiser with newer sponsors such as Byju’s, Crypto.com, and Qatar Energy. Participants were asked to identify official sponsors of the event. Some respondents completed a survey that listed only sponsors, while others saw sponsors alongside competing brands that were not official sponsors. By comparing responses across the two conditions, we could determine whether the presence of competitor brands influenced people’s ability to correctly recall sponsors.

      We found that the presence of competitor brands in the survey had almost no effect on awareness of newer sponsors. (The recall rates were 50.02% versus 49.7%.) However, it significantly reduced measured awareness of long-established sponsors. When competitors were not included in the survey, established sponsors achieved recall rates of about 73%. When competitors were included, recall fell to about 60%—a decline of roughly 17%. Why does that happen? We believe that years of exposure to long-tenured sponsors creates a complex network of associations in people’s minds, making them more likely to confuse a sponsor with a competing brand when both are presented together. As a result, commonly used awareness surveys may underestimate the value and recognition of long-term sponsorships.

      Second, partnerships create strong, lasting mental associations—and that can contribute to mental clutter.

      Our research shows that sponsorships create residual recall that lasts for years after contracts end. We call this shared brand equity: a two-way path between your brand and the event sponsored. Thinking of Omega as a timepiece option? The Olympics might come to mind. If you are Omega, this is valuable connectivity in the consumer’s mind—one that lasts long after the Games end. If you are a rival watch brand taking over from an established competitor like Omega in the international ecosystem, you’re fighting against consumers’ tendency to associate Omega with the event long after the sponsorship has ended. It is like swimming upstream…for years.

      Counterintuitively, our research shows that consumer confusion over what brand sponsored an event can actually grow in the months after the competition. Brands typically measure recall by doing surveys just before and just after an event. Our work across a range of sports shows that when a brand takes over a sponsorship, at the time of the event the recall of the new sponsor was on average 20%, whereas recall of the old sponsor was 23%. Six months out from the event, with media attention gone, recall dropped to 12% for the new replacing sponsors, while mistaken recall of the old sponsors had resurfaced at 40%.

      This problem is worse when the old sponsor had been in place for many years. Consider an anecdotal example: Heinz Field, for 20 years the home of the Pittsburgh Steelers. It was renamed Acrisure Stadium in 2022, but if you spend time in Pittsburgh, you’ll quickly understand how many people still refer to it as Heinz Field. Even strong exposure during an event does not fundamentally shift how memory operates, as the mind naturally revisits past associations.

      This means that brands in sponsoring ecosystems are not only competing for consumer awareness against other brands in the moment, but also against a collection of past sponsors.

      What Managers Should Do Differently

      If exposure and recall are flawed goals for sponsoring success, what should replace them?

      Brand managers should measure what matters to the brand and what sponsoring can deliver brilliantly: emotional connection, brand understanding, quality perceptions, and reciprocity.

      In keeping with “what gets measured, gets managed” thinking, and the research we have on sponsoring, I have identified four sets of practices that companies can use to increase connection, memorability and reduce the unhelpful focus on exposure.

      Create signature experiences.

      Distinctive experiences anchor a brand in the consumer’s mind. For example, the tradition of dumping the contents of the Gatorade cooler onto the coach following a football win brings inestimable brand value.

      While the tradition began as a playful one-off, Gatorade supports the dunk with special coolers featuring extra handles on the bottom to make it easier for players to lift and tip. Bettors now wager more than $1 million on what color or flavor of Gatorade will be used for the celebratory shower. The Gatorade tradition, like the best signature experiences, began organically—but it’s grown with support from the brand, without being co-opted by the brand.

      Build from what you bring, not what you receive.

      LVMH product demonstrations at the 2024 Olympics orient to what the luxury house offers the event, not what the event offers them. As creative partner and co-producer, they dressed the performing artists and French athletes, developed the trunks to hold the medals, and designed the medals as if they were jewelry. They rightly claim that they crafted the Paris Games to embody their mission. This was accomplished within Olympic rules that do not allow brand advertising in competition areas.

      Sponsoring brands should not choose from the available assets of the sport property; they should imagine their own. Accepting generic sport property assets, such as a half-time shoot-out in basketball, may drive brand exposure but will blend the brand with every other that has utilized that type of sport communication tactic.

      Integrate the brand experientially.

      While Mattel has been an active collaborator with NASCAR with their Hot Wheels die-cast lineup, toy integration with sport leveled up with Lego’s F1 partnership. From building the trophies for the British Grand Prix to having fans build their own mini race car, test it in a wind simulator, and race it at Symphony Park in Charlotte, NC, Lego has taught a master class in experiential engagement.

      Yes, integrated merchandising is part of the story, but the two-way street for Lego and F1 is lined with new fans for the sport. The company is a case study for “less sponsorship as advertising” and more sponsorship as authentic engagement. Treating sponsoring as advertising does not take full advantage of the opportunities sponsoring holds.

      Use time strategically.

      Typically, brand managers focus their collateral advertising of a sponsored event on the lead-up to, and at the time of the event. For long-term strategies and spaced events, more collateral spending is needed during the hiatus between events.

      A rare but award-winning ad of this type by Visa aired during the Super Bowl in 2004. The off-season Olympics ad showed the women’s volleyball team trudging through snow to play. The tag line: “Just a reminder, in the dead of winter, that the Summer Olympics are right around the corner, including the U.S. Beach Volleyball team. But if you want to see them, grab your Visa card.” Hiatus communications fight against sponsor confusion and highlight commitment to the sport, which circles back to authenticity and trust.

      . . . 

      Sponsorship assessment is overly dependent on brand name/logo exposure as a KPI. Further, the legacy measure of “advertising value equivalency” continues to give a false sense of financial worth in sponsoring. These metrics fail to capture what matters most: how consumers perceive, organize, and remember brands over time.

      More importantly, new research demonstrates that brand impressions are now confused due to the cacophony of impressions in the audience’s mind from decades of sponsoring as an associative marketing strategy. Real value emerges when brands move beyond visibility toward meaningful embeddedness. Developing the best that sponsorship has to offer–true partnership–holds more promise. It requires departing from legacy performance targets and embracing experiential engagement.

      The brands that are truly successful at the upcoming World Cup and next year’s Olympics will not be those seen most often, but those remembered most meaningfully.
aiSummary: 本文认为，品牌在体育赞助中过度依赖过时的曝光度指标，而应专注于打造真实、令人难忘的体验，以建立持久的情感联系和信任。
oneSentenceSummary: 本文认为，品牌在体育赞助中过度依赖过时的曝光度指标，而应专注于打造真实、令人难忘的体验，以建立持久的情感联系和信任。
summary: 本文批评了将广告价值等价物和标志曝光度作为体育赞助关键绩效指标的主流做法。作者基于超过 25 年的学术研究，论证了这些指标存在缺陷，因为消费者的记忆常常被混乱的赞助生态系统以及过往赞助商留下的长期心理联想所干扰。文章展示的研究表明，在回忆调查中，竞争对手品牌的出现会显著降低对长期赞助商的认知度，而对旧赞助商的错误记忆可能在赛事结束数月后重新浮现。为解决这些问题，作者提出了四个战略转变：打造标志性体验（例如佳得乐的庆祝扣篮）、从品牌为赛事带来的价值出发（例如 LVMH 在 2024 年奥运会）、将品牌进行体验式整合（例如乐高与 F1 的合作），以及通过非赛季传播策略性地利用时间。核心信息是，真正的赞助价值源于有意义的融入和真诚的合作伙伴关系，而非仅仅追求曝光度。

mainPoints.point: 广告价值等价物等曝光度指标已经过时且具有误导性。
mainPoints.explanation: 这些指标未能捕捉消费者实际如何感知和记忆品牌，导致对财务价值的错误认知，并使品牌关注点从真正的联系转向了曝光度。

mainPoints.point: 消费者对赞助商的记忆被混乱的生态系统和过往联想系统性地干扰。
mainPoints.explanation: 研究表明，在回忆调查中，竞争对手品牌的出现会降低对长期赞助商的认知度，而对旧赞助商的错误记忆可能在赛事结束数月后增加，从而削弱了新赞助协议的价值。

mainPoints.point: 品牌应衡量情感联系、品牌理解和互惠关系，而非曝光度。
mainPoints.explanation: 这些指标能更好地捕捉赞助所能带来的真实价值，例如建立信任，以及让消费者因欣赏品牌对其所关心事物的支持而产生互惠感。

mainPoints.point: 标志性体验和体验式整合能创造更令人难忘、更真实的品牌联想。
mainPoints.explanation: 佳得乐的庆祝扣篮、LVMH 作为创意合作伙伴的角色以及乐高在 F1 中的动手实践激活等案例，展示了品牌如何超越常规广告，创造独特而持久的印象。

mainPoints.point: 策略性地利用时间，包括非赛季传播，有助于维持品牌记忆并对抗混淆。
mainPoints.explanation: Visa 在超级碗期间投放奥运会休赛期广告是一个罕见的例子，它利用休赛期传播提醒消费者品牌对某项运动的承诺，从而强化了真实性和信任。

keyQuotes: 在即将到来的世界杯和明年的奥运会上，真正成功的品牌不会是那些曝光率最高的，而是那些被最有意义地记住的。
在一个以消费者信任脆弱为特征的 AI 时代，体育合作伙伴关系提供了真实性，并由此建立了信任。
当品牌超越曝光度，走向有意义的融入时，真正的价值才会显现。
